setting.note: Austen builds her world out of invented houses set on real English ground. Longbourn and Meryton are fictional, but she plants them in Hertfordshire, four-and-twenty miles from London, near the real market towns of the county. The Gardiners' home in Gracechurch Street, by Cheapside, is a real City address; Mr. Collins's Hunsford is sited near the real Westerham in Kent; the militia camp at Brighton is the real resort; and the Derbyshire tour runs through the actual Peak, past Matlock, Dovedale and Chatsworth, where readers have looked for Pemberley ever since. The map draws the arc of Elizabeth's England, from a Hertfordshire drawing-room to the great house in the Derbyshire hills.

rights.originalWork: Public domain. Jane Austen, first published 1813; the author died in 1817.
rights.territoryNotes: Public domain in the United States (published 1813) and in the United Kingdom and the EU, where the author died more than 70 years ago (1817).
rights.attribution: Jane Austen, Pride and Prejudice (1813). Text from Project Gutenberg.

palette.note: The scarlet of the militia officers who camp near Meryton and then at Brighton, the red coats that turn the younger Bennets' heads and carry off Lydia. Clears WCAG AA on paper.
